headline: Birth of Ellen Boddington Baumler, Montana's Interpretive Historian, 1949
body:
On the eighth day of March, in the year of our Lord nineteen hundred and forty-nine, a soul destined to illuminate the forgotten annals of the American West first drew breath in the distant climes of Kansas City. This was Ellen Boddington, who, by the grace of matrimony, would become Baumler, and by the tireless application of intellect and passion, would emerge as one of Montana’s most revered chroniclers of its storied past. Her early years were marked by a dedication to scholarly pursuits, culminating in the attainment of a doctoral degree from the venerable University of Kansas in the year of our Lord nineteen hundred and eighty-five, a testament to a mind steeped in the classical traditions of English, classics, and history.

It was in the year of our Lord nineteen hundred and eighty-eight that the currents of fate, or perhaps the allure of the vast, untamed landscapes, drew her westward from Tucson, Arizona, to the burgeoning capital of Helena, Montana. Here, amidst the echoes of prospectors and pioneers, her true vocation began to unfold. In the year of our Lord nineteen hundred and ninety-two, the Montana Historical Society, with prescient wisdom, appointed her as the state’s inaugural Interpretive Historian, a role she would grace for a quarter-century, until her retirement in the year of our Lord two thousand and eighteen. Yet, even in retirement, the spirit of inquiry and the urge to narrate remained undimmed, for the true historian finds no cessation to their sacred task.

Her tenure was a veritable golden age for the elucidation of Montana’s heritage. With an unwavering commitment to historic preservation, she embarked upon a monumental endeavor, authoring more than sixteen hundred interpretive signs and historical markers that now dot the vast expanse of the state, each a silent sentinel whispering tales of yesteryear to the passing traveler. Her diligent efforts also saw countless Montana locales enshrined upon the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring their legacy for generations yet unborn. Beyond these tangible markers, her pen flowed freely, yielding a prolific collection of books and articles, many of which delved into the spectral tapestry of Montana’s haunted locales, earning her the affectionate moniker, “Queen of Halloween.” She possessed a singular gift for unearthing the human dramas that lay beneath the dust of ages, believing, as she once conveyed through the words of another, that “All good things, he wrote, come by grace and grace comes by art and art does not come easy.” Indeed, the artistry of history, in her hands, was a grace bestowed upon all Montanans.

Her legacy is not merely etched in stone or bound in printed pages, but woven into the very fabric of Montana’s collective memory. Through her tireless advocacy, her engaging narratives, and her profound understanding of the past, Ellen Boddington Baumler transformed the dry facts of history into living, breathing stories, ensuring that the spirit of Montana’s pioneers, its triumphs, and its tribulations, would forever resonate across its majestic mountains and sweeping plains. She departed this mortal coil on the sixteenth day of December, in the year of our Lord two thousand and twenty-three, leaving behind a rich inheritance of knowledge and a profound appreciation for the enduring power of history.
